sections.explainer: Standalone services whose entire product is memory for AI agents. They sit between your agent code and durable storage — handling extraction, governance, contradiction resolution, and recall via API. The "database vendor" analogue for the memory tier: you bring an agent, they bring durability.

Memory subsystems shipped inside a broader agent orchestration framework. Memory isn't the product — the framework is — and the memory module is one component alongside tool-calling, planning, and routing. Composition over best-in-class: convenient when you've already chosen the framework, limiting when you outgrow what's built in.

First-party memory features built into a foundation-model provider's chat product or cloud platform. The provider owns user, model, and memory together — tight integration with their product, but no portability across vendors and limited control for builders. The "memory feature in ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini / Copilot" tier.

Memory features baked directly into AI coding products — Devin, Replit Agent, Codex CLI, v0, Bolt.new, Lovable. The memory is part of the agent UX rather than an SDK or framework you compose with. Distinct from file-backed paradigms because here the agent is autonomous; distinct from platform-provider memory because the product is the coding workflow itself.

Browser-as-agent products where memory persists across browsing sessions and the agent operates on the user's browser context. Distinct from coding-agent memory because the environment is web pages and tabs; distinct from personal AI because the corpus is browsing history and tab content rather than notes.

Consumer-facing memory products centered on the individual user's life or notes — screen recordings, conversations, networked-thought note apps, second-brain tools. The agent layer is secondary; the primary product is the personal corpus and the UX of recalling from it. Where memory becomes a consumer-product category.

Hardware-tethered memory products that capture audio and ambient context continuously — pendants, recorders, meeting bots, AI-augmented note-takers. Memory is the primary product feature; the pitch is "remember everything you say and hear." Privacy, battery life, and always-on capture are the engineering frontier.

Memory systems with a paper and a reference implementation, often introducing distinctive architectural ideas — graph + temporal, OS-style hierarchies, three-phase consolidation, surprise-based gating. Not yet at production scale, but where new architectures are first tested. Many of the production systems above are direct descendants of entries here.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: Recent papers (mostly 2024–2026) proposing novel memory approaches with no shipped product or widely-used implementation yet. The frontier — most won't pan out, some will define the next generation. Read this section as ideas to test, not tools to adopt. Sub-grouped below by the kind of memory the paper targets.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: Methods for storing and retrieving discrete facts — entities, events, observations — typically as tokenisable text passed in and out of the LLM. The dominant paradigm; this is where most memory research currently lives.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.subsection: — Experiential & procedural memory (agent learning from experience)
sections.explainer: Methods focused on remembering how to do things — successful trajectories, failed attempts, learned skills, plan templates. The unit of memory is a procedure or an experience, not a fact. The natural home of agent self-improvement work.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: Methods for managing what's in the model's context window during a single task — compression, eviction, recurrent state, segment-level recurrence. The "active" tier of memory, analogous to RAM in cognitive-science framings. Closely related to long-context architectures.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.subsection: — Parametric & latent memory (memory in weights / KV / hidden states)
sections.explainer: Methods that put memory into the model's parameters or latent representations rather than discrete text. Includes memory-augmented architectures, KV-cache compression, and knowledge editing. Where "memory" stops being external storage and becomes part of the model's internal state.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: Architectures that learn an internal model of the environment and use it as a memory of "how the world works" — agents plan and learn by imagining rollouts inside this model rather than recalling discrete facts. Adjacent to LeCun's JEPA programme and to RL world-model lines (Dreamer, Genie). Memory here is a learned dynamics function, not a database.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: Methods that prevent a model from forgetting earlier tasks when trained on new ones. Distinct from "memory" in the agent sense — the model itself is the memory, and the question is how to update its weights without losing prior capabilities. Tightly coupled to neural-architecture work; relevant whenever you fine-tune sequentially.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: RL agents that maintain explicit episodic memory of past trajectories — the agent recalls similar past states/actions to make better decisions now. Distinct from policy-only RL or world-model RL: memory is the policy mechanism. Where neuroscience-inspired memory work most directly meets RL.

sections.section: Recent method papers — theorized, no distinct product
sections.explainer: Approaches that import structures from classical cognitive architectures (Soar, ACT-R, complementary learning systems) into LLM-based agents. Bridge between cognitive science and modern AI memory.

Retrieval techniques (RAG variants) used as the recall mechanism for a memory system. The retrieval method is the memory architecture's read path. Where the line between "RAG" and "memory" blurs — both store knowledge externally and pull it on demand, but these techniques specifically address the memory-shaped retrieval problem.

Memory implemented as plain-text files under version control, injected at session start. No database, no extraction LLM, no vector index — just human-readable rules and notes. Optimal when memory is curated, low-volume, and benefits from human review (CLAUDE.md, Cursor rules, Windsurf rules). The opposite philosophical pole from extraction-and-storage memory layers.

The specific memory features available inside Claude Code — what this CLI session uses to remember things across runs. Distinct from the "Anthropic Claude Memory" entry above because this is the CLI / IDE-integration tier (auto-memory directory, MCP plugins, MEMORY.md indices), not the consumer chat product.

Graph databases (RDF, property-graph) used as the substrate for AI memory. Not memory products themselves, but where graph-shaped memory products like Zep/Graphiti, Cognee, and Neo4j-backed agents typically sit. Worth understanding the substrate even if you don't operate one directly — the choice of graph DB constrains what kinds of memory queries are cheap.

Vector databases used as the substrate for semantic-recall memory. Same role as the knowledge-graph platforms — substrate beneath the memory product. Most dedicated memory layers are built on one or more of these; choice of vector DB sets the cost / latency / scale envelope of the memory product above it.

Enterprise search products that solve memory-shaped problems for organisations rather than for agents — KCS-style governance, per-user knowledge graphs, curated bibliographic databases. Worth knowing because they handle organisational scale and governance issues that agent memory products are still figuring out.

AI products and platforms whose memory features are tuned for a specific vertical — healthcare, legal, science, gaming, robotics, customer support. Distinct from horizontal memory infrastructure because the memory model is shaped by domain semantics (causal biology graphs, matter-context, NPC personalities, scene graphs, ticket histories) rather than generic facts.
Tools where the memory layer is the architectural differentiator — persistent causal graphs, claim-to-sentence provenance, experiment-loop memory — rather than plain LLM-over-papers retrieval.

Healthcare AI products with distinctive memory layers — longitudinal patient stores, EMR-filtered retrieval, ambient-plus-historical reconciliation. Excludes plain LLM-over-EHR products without a documented memory architecture.

Legal AI products with persistent matter / firm / institutional memory. T1 entries store decisions, accepted positions, and prior precedent that actively influence future outputs — not just CRM-style document lookup.

CX, voice, and contact-center AI products. T1 = system writes durable, queryable customer-level state that the AI retrieves autonomously. T2 = well-integrated CRM reader without independent memory layer.

AI products for game NPCs, character chat, interactive fiction, and AI companions. Memory layer encodes character knowledge (what each character knows), personality continuity, and persistent world state.

Memory architectures for embodied AI — robot manipulation, navigation, autonomous driving, humanoids. Distinct paradigm: spatial maps, scene graphs, world-model latents, episodic trajectories. Memory grounded in physical state rather than language.

AI tutors, language-learning apps, and teacher productivity tools. Memory shape varies sharply: SRS state (Quizlet, Duolingo), pronunciation profiles (ELSA), curriculum mastery graphs (Khanmigo), per-student session logs (SchoolAI), or no student-facing memory at all (MagicSchool — by design). The architectural divide is whether memory lives in the AI tutor, the teacher dashboard, or a structured curriculum — three answers to "who carries the model of the learner."

The infrastructure used to measure every memory system in the table. LongMemEval, LoCoMo, ConvoMem, BABILong, MADial-Bench, and similar. Not memory systems themselves — but essential for reading the benchmark claims in the rest of the table critically. Each benchmark tests a slightly different thing (single-session recall, multi-session continuity, temporal reasoning, multi-hop, adversarial).

Tools for tracing, drift-detection, and debugging memory in production — Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, custom poisoning detectors, memory-quality dashboards. Adjacent to LLMOps but specifically scoped to memory health: who wrote what, when, did it diverge from the corpus, did anyone slip in poisoned facts.

Products that secure memory writes and reads — poisoning protection, differential-privacy wrappers, memory access control, audit logging for memory mutations. The compliance and safety tier for systems that retain user data. Emerging category — most production memory systems treat this as someone else's problem.

sections.section: Theoretical / informal — ideas without a paper
sections.explainer: Ideas that exist only as tweets, blog posts, talks, or RFCs — no paper, no implementation. The earliest stage of the funnel: pure speculation, but where the next generation of papers is seeded. Populate with named concepts as you encounter them.

Fine-tuning platforms, RLHF stacks, dataset stores, label tools, training-data versioning, distributed training systems, and synthetic-data generation — the layer underneath every memory model. Added in the 2026-05-12 scope expansion: many of these systems touch memory-relevant artefacts (long-context training runs, dataset versioning for replay buffers, RLHF reward models that condition on memory). Coverage here is broad-not-deep — read as a substrate map, not a head-to-head comparison.

Vector databases, hybrid search engines, embedding services, and search-as-a-service platforms that are not primarily pitched as memory products. Distinct from the 'Vector-database infrastructure' section, which collects substrates known to underlie memory products; this section catches the broader retrieval-infra layer including pure search platforms (Algolia, Elastic, Typesense, Meilisearch), embedding API services (Cohere Embed, Voyage, Jina, Nomic), and managed-cloud SKUs of vector DBs already in the catalog.

Agent orchestration, tool-calling, and workflow-engine frameworks whose primary value is not memory — even when they ship a memory adapter, the framework is the product. Distinct from 'Framework-embedded memory' (where the row characterises the framework's own memory subsystem) — this section's rows characterise the framework's orchestration / agent abstraction, with memory as a downstream adapter concern. Some entries here are cross-listings of rows that also appear in Framework-embedded memory.

Inference-as-a-service platforms (Replicate, Modal, Together, Anyscale, Baseten, Fireworks) and LLM gateways / proxies / routers (LiteLLM, OpenRouter, Portkey, Helicone Gateway). The layer beneath every memory product that calls an LLM — relevant because the gateway's caching, observability, and multi-tenancy stories interact with the memory system's recall path.

Hosted embedding APIs and reranker services (Cohere Embed, Voyage AI, OpenAI Embeddings, Jina, Nomic, Mixedbread). Distinct from open-weight embedding model entries elsewhere — this section characterises the managed-API tier. Every vector-recall memory product chooses an embedding service (or a self-hosted alternative); this is the menu.

LLM/agent evaluation, tracing, and observability platforms (LangSmith, LangFuse, Helicone, Phoenix, Galileo, Patronus, Confident AI, TruEra). Distinct from the memory-specific 'Memory observability & monitoring' section (which focuses on memory-quality dashboards, poisoning detection). This section is for general-purpose eval/observe tooling that any agent — memory-enabled or not — would adopt.

User-facing agentic IDEs, terminal harnesses, and agent-runtime products — the layer where the harness is the agent. Distinct from 'Coding-agent memory' (which is the memory subsystem of these products, e.g. CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, Devin Knowledge) and from 'Agent frameworks (no first-party memory layer)' (which is import-and-compose libraries). Added in the 2026-05-13 Round-11 sweep: this is the consumer-product surface — Kiro, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, Claude Code, Codex CLI, Aider, Cline, Goose, Charm Crush, JetBrains AI / Junie, GitHub Copilot Workspace, Amazon Q Developer, etc. Coverage is broad-and-shallow on the harness layer; memory-layer rows remain canonical for the memory framing.

Substrate and harness for agents that operate keyboards, mice, browsers, and full OS sessions. Distinct from Browser-agent memory (which focused on memory persisted across browsing sessions) and from Agent IDEs & coding harnesses (where the agent's environment is a code editor). Added in the 2026-05-13 Round-12 sweep: OpenAI Operator, Browser Use, Skyvern, Stagehand, Bytebot, Hyperbrowser, Steel.dev, Magnitude, Lutra AI, Highlight AI, Claude for Chrome, Project Mariner, UI-TARS. Coverage is broad-and-shallow on the harness layer; the Anthropic Computer Use API row (which is the underlying substrate Anthropic itself ships) remains canonical in Agent IDEs & coding harnesses for that framing.

Developer platforms for building AI voice agents — STT + LLM + TTS pipelines, telephony adapters, call orchestration. Distinct from Voice-first / wearable AI memory (which is the consumer-product layer — Limitless, Plaud, Rewind) and from Vertical / domain-specific AI memory — Customer support / voice agent memory (which is the CRM-shaped memory layer inside CX products). Added in the 2026-05-13 Round-12 sweep: Vapi, Retell, Bland, Synthflow, ElevenLabs Conversational AI, OpenAI Realtime, Pipecat, LiveKit Agents, Cartesia Sonic, Hume EVI, Speechmatics Flow, CallRail, Vonage AI Studio. Memory in these systems is mostly per-call session state; the interesting differentiation is latency, model integration depth, and telephony breadth — not memory architecture.

Full-stack robotics platforms — foundation models (VLA / diffusion-policy), manipulation skills, planners, sim2real stacks. Distinct from the existing Vertical / domain-specific AI memory — Robotics / autonomous-driving / embodied memory subsection (which collects memory architectures for embodied AI as a research-and-product theme). This section is the platform-layer roll-up: humanoid makers (Figure, 1X, Apptronik, Agility, Sanctuary, K-Scale), pure-play FM labs (Physical Intelligence π, TRI LBM, Google DeepMind Gemini Robotics, NVIDIA GR00T), warehouse-pick specialists (Covariant, Pickle), and the OSS substrate (LeRobot, OpenMind OM1). Memory in these systems is mostly parametric (in weights) or trajectory-buffer; the agent runtime layer is the model + simulator + on-robot compute stack.
